titleStmt.title: The Counselor of Embassy in the United Kingdom ( (Bucknell) ) to the Secretary of State

div.head: The Counselor of Embassy in the United Kingdom ( Bucknell ) to the Secretary of State
div.p: 4163. From Schoenfeld.Rudolf E. Schoenfeld, Counselor of Embassy to the Yugoslav Government in Exile at London. Your 3971, May 18, midnight.Not printed. I have just seen King Peter. He tells me British insists that he form a government today. There will be questions regarding Yugoslavia in the House of Commons tomorrow and Mr. Churchill feels he must be in a position to answer them.
King Peter says he has been conferring all day with Yugoslav personalities here but still does not know whether he can form a government today.

The present plan is to form a small Cabinet and a “resistance committee”. The Cabinet would consist of 3 or 4 persons including one Croatian, one Slovene and one Serb. Mihailovic would be dropped as Minister of War but would remain CinC.Commander in Chief. Tito would not be represented in the government. Both Mihailovic and Tito would be invited to send representatives to the resistance committee.

As for the composition of the Cabinet, King Peter says Ban Subasic is willing to join. A suitable Slovene is also available, either KotzePossibly Edvard Kotzbek. or CzokPossibly Ivan Pchok. or Snoj.Frano Snoj. A suitable Serb has not yet been secured. King Peter would like JovanovicSlobodan Jovanovich, distinguished Serbian scholar who served as Prime Minister of the Yugoslav Government in Exile from January 12, 1942, until August 10, 1943. and is seeing him later this evening. He feels an important Serb personality is essential among other reasons to offset the effect on Serb opinion of the change of status of Mihailovic. [Schoenfeld.]
